topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my screen, a jarring intrusion into the otherwise quiet afternoon. A profile picture loaded: a grinning face I hadn't seen in fifteen years. My jaw tightened, the muscles in my neck corded with tension. It was him. Mark. The boy who used to dismantle my confidence with casual cruelty in the fifth grade. A slow, hot flush crept up my neck, staining my cheeks a deep crimson. I didn't even need to click on it; the urge to slam my laptop shut, to throw the device across the room, was almost overwhelming. I stared at the little 'Add Friend' button, my thumb hovering over it like a coiled spring. What was he expecting? A response? I felt a sudden, sharp need to scream into a pillow, to shatter the silence with sheer, unadulterated fury.

[story 2]

I reread the request three times, my breath catching in short, shallow gasps. It was impossible. After all this time, after the years of building a life that felt solid and earned, that shadow reappeared, seeking reentry. A tremor ran through my hands as I gripped the mouse. My knuckles were white against the plastic. Every nerve ending felt hyper-alert, buzzing with a frantic, unwelcome energy. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap office chair groaning in protest beneath me. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat—more of a growl than a word. I furiously typed a message, each keystroke imbued with a brittle, icy edge. I needed to make it clear that this intrusion would not be tolerated.

[story 3]

The email landed in my inbox with a dull thud. Seeing his name, the familiar knot of resentment tightened painfully in my chest. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of my small apartment. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic drumbeat echoing the rising heat in my core. I walked over to the window, staring out at the indifferent cityscape. My breath hitched. It wasn't just anger; it was a deep, visceral churning, a need to physically lash out at the source of this unwelcome contact. I clenched my fists so tightly that my fingernails dug crescent moons into my palms. I wanted to dismantle the screen, to erase the very presence of that request from existence.
